data.title: McDonald's: The Stealth AI Dividend Growth Machine To Buy On The Dip
data.description:
When I typically think of McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD)(NEOE:MCDS:CA), AI is one of the last things that comes to mind. Instead, I think of my fond memories as a child playing on their playgrounds and eating their Happy Meals, and today as a parent taking my children there and watching them devour double cheeseburgers and enjoy their crispy fries. However, the reality is that MCD is one of the best-positioned companies to profit from the AI boom, and it does not appear that the market fully appreciates that yet. In this article, I will explain why and share why this dividend growth machine is a great buy on the dip.

MCD Business Snapshot

MCD is a popular fast food company that primarily focuses on owning quality real estate and franchising out its storefronts while leveraging its immense scale and treasure trove of data to implement superior and highly targeted marketing, optimize its core menu, and deliver efficient service along with tasty food. Recently, the company has been performing fairly well, as in Q2 its comparable sales across the globe grew by 3.8%; in the U.S., they were up by 2.5%. Overall, sales were up 8% or 6% on a constant currency basis, and operating income on a consolidated basis was up by 11%. The company is leaning aggressively into leveraging technology and scaling the use of AI tools as a core part of their tech stack to drive continued profitability growth, both in increasing top-line sales as well as improving operating efficiencies.

Why McDonald’s Is A Hidden AI Powerhouse

The key to understanding why MCD is so well positioned to benefit from the AI boom is to understand both the nature of how AI works and also the industry in which MCD operates, and the business model that it employs. AI is largely driven by the quality and size of data available to the model builder. Therefore, generally speaking, the more data and the better quality that data is, the better the machine learning model will be.

Meanwhile, MCD operates in the fast food industry, where the primary concerns for consumers tend to be getting quality food as efficiently as possible on both a time and cost basis. This stands in contrast to sit-down restaurant businesses like Cracker Barrel (CBRL) and Olive Garden (DRI), which tend to prioritize experience much more since they are going to the restaurant to sit down and enjoy a date or other forms of human-to-human interaction. This increases the opportunities for automation and the fast food industry relative to sit-down restaurants.

Finally, MCD operates a real estate-heavy model, as they are as much a real estate business as they are a fast food restaurant. Given their immense size and scale and their ability to acquire high-quality and well-located real estate, MCD's real estate empire also positions them well to benefit from AI. How this all works together is that MCD, through its huge real estate portfolio, can build a model that helps it to determine and plot a good growth strategy in which it will be able to use performance data for its existing properties to determine the best places to acquire new properties to install new storefronts. Given that their data on this front is likely vastly superior to any of their competitors, as AI tools progress, this should only further accentuate their advantage in being able to find the best properties to own.

McDonald's began building the infrastructure to become an AI powerhouse back in December 2023, when it announced a multi-year partnership with Alphabet (GOOGL), bringing in its generative AI and edge computing capabilities into McDonald's storefronts across the world. Additionally, there would be a dedicated GOOGL Cloud team positioned near McDonald's Speedy Labs in Chicago. This initiative was designed to help the company reduce equipment downtime, improve the efficiencies of its restaurant crews, and ultimately leverage data analytics and other AI applications using GOOGL distributed cloud hardware and the restaurants to deliver better food more efficiently.

Earlier this year, it took another step forward in its AI initiatives by deploying internet-connected kitchen equipment across its 43,000 restaurants and also incorporating AI into its drive-through and giving its store managers AI-powered tools. All this was designed to help reduce employee stress, spot equipment issues before the equipment would fail, and also to get an overall real-time view, even for franchisees, into how their individual restaurants are operating. It will also generate AI virtual manager capabilities by automating previously time-intensive tasks like scheduling worker shifts and handling other administrative tasks, thereby freeing storefront managers to focus on maximizing service, employee mentorship, and hiring.

This will ultimately improve the customer experience as well by leading to less stressed employees, increasing the productivity of employees, and thereby reducing wait times, and also reducing customer experiences with various machines failing, including MCD ice cream machines, which have a reputation for breaking down a lot. Additionally, these AI systems are being installed through edge computing systems, which means that the computing power will be installed in the restaurants themselves rather than at some remote location in the cloud. This means that these AI applications will be more efficient and further enhance the benefits of this AI. As the Wall Street Journal reported, these initiatives make MCD an early mover in its industry, which will give it an advantage on top of its immense scale that already gives it an edge.

Given that about 95% of MCD franchise locations are franchised, this makes the MCD App and loyalty program particularly well-suited for the company's AI initiatives. This is because digital infrastructure is much easier to roll out across an already well-established app-based loyalty program that minimizes the need for training and interfacing with franchisees, rather than having to go to each store and teach them how to use robotics technology. So I would expect software and app-based AI enhancements to continue to take the lead, though eventually robotics will likely also be implemented; however, that side of the coin will likely be more challenging for MCD given its heavy reliance on franchisees.

The Power Of Data: 175 Million Loyalty Members And Counting

Additionally, MCD's huge Loyalty Membership Program with 175-plus million members today and a path to 250 million by 2027 further positions them to dominate in the age of AI. This is because their membership program is highly digital-centric, with them pushing almost all activity to the app.

In addition to its ambitious growth goals for loyalty users, the company is also targeting $45 billion in annual company-wide sales that are directly attributable to loyalty members by 2027, which would be $20 billion higher when it set the goal in late 2023, which will be skiffly higher than the over $20 billion goal that it initially had in late 2023, and also well above the goal of about $30 billion when it updated its goal in 2024. This demonstrates that the loyalty member program is bringing on significant new revenue for the company, validating, at least to some extent, the effectiveness of these AI-driven initiatives.

The reason this is so advantageous for them is that it results in instant and highly clean data collection from an enormous swath of people that span virtually every demographic and many different geographies. Using such a huge amount of reliable data that is also collected with time and geographic stamps means that they can project with a high degree of accuracy what sort of targeted promotions will be most effective in maximizing transaction volume in terms of both the number of visits and ticket size.

Additionally, they will be able to tailor these to each individual member quite efficiently as they categorize them across geographic and demographic labels and even target them to weather conditions, time of year, and time of day. This should also lead to significant marketing savings for them over peers, as pushing promotions via apps and emails is far more cost-effective, especially when highly individualized and targeted like this, compared to targeting consumers via traditional routes of television, direct mail, radio, or internet banner and video advertising.

If MCD can effectively grow loyalty member sales from $30 billion in 2024 to a 50% increase to $45 billion in 2027, it would serve as a strong indicator that its AI-powered marketing initiatives are paying off. This would not only benefit franchisees, but it would also benefit MCD's bottom line as well, given that royalties as a percentage of sales are a big source of revenue for MCD, thereby incremental ticket and visit growth from the loyalty program would drive strong bottom-line growth for the company as well.

Automation And Efficiency: The Next Frontier Of Fast Food

Finally, their immense scale and their positioning within the fast food industry position them to benefit much more from automation than sit-down peers, while their huge scale means that they will likely be able to deploy robotics and other automation tools much better than their smaller competitors without hurting the customer experience much at all. In fact, robotics may only enhance it, given the priority for quality, efficiency, and low cost in the experience, all of which automation should enhance.

Of course, not every AI initiative is guaranteed to be a success. In fact, MCD already tried a partnership with International Business Machines (IBM) to have an automated drive-through voice system, but ended it in 2024 after having unsatisfactory accuracy results. Though it does plan to continue exploring automated voice solutions for drive-through in the future with other partners. Thus far, MCD seems to have achieved bigger returns from its maintenance, labor planning, and order verification AI systems, such as those it has implemented through its partnership with GOOGL, rather than from direct consumer-facing automations.

That said, as AI technology improves, MCD should benefit from this as well. And given its scale and vast financial resources, especially compared to smaller peers, it stands to be one of the early beneficiaries of these advancements. The company already has a prototype of a highly automated storefront in Fort Worth, Texas, where its order-ahead lane has been in operation since late 2022. It is driven by app-ordering ahead of time, uses automated location signals to tell consumers where to go, and then uses a conveyor to deliver the food to consumers. A big benefit of this system is that these improvements can easily scale with a digital app and overall corporate-wide AI stack, rather than requiring heavy investment in robotics.

Valuation: A Dividend Growth Machine On Sale

Given that MCD is so well-positioned to benefit from the rise of AI, we think that today it makes for a decent investment, given that its stock price recently pulled back even at a time when the overall market is near all-time highs. In fact, MCD's valuation is pretty compelling given that it offers a dividend yield that is more than two times what is offered by the S&P 500. Additionally, its price-to-earnings ratio is only slightly above its historic average...

...and its earnings-per-share growth and dividend-per-share growth outlook is strong, with a 10% expected earnings-per-share CAGR through 2029 and a 13% dividend-per-share CAGR expected through the end of the decade as well.

This indicates that simply assuming a constant price-to-earnings multiple, MCD is positioned to deliver double-digit annualized total returns.

However, I think that as the benefits of AI become increasingly evident over the course of the next five years for MCD, it will likely command a much higher multiple as its dominance in this space becomes more pronounced, and its treasure trove of data, its huge membership on its app, and its success in the digital space. Its scale in real estate advantages will give it many other options to expand as a business to drive additional incremental growth.

Risks And Investor Takeaway

The main risk facing MCD today, in my view, is the renewed emphasis on health being in food and, in particular, the pushback against highly processed foods, being led by the Trump administration's Make America Healthy Again initiative under RFK's direction. A secondary major risk is that MCD could have bad headlines from food going bad in some of its chains or even lawsuits due to the relatively unhealthy nature of its food or simply just continue to fall out of favor with future young generations if it fails to effectively market and innovate itself to appeal to them.

However, this latter risk, I think, is largely mitigated by the increasing power of AI applications to model the desires and effectively mold the menu and marketing campaigns to appeal to virtually any demographic, and ultimately, the efficiency, affordability, and taste of the food, if optimized effectively via AI, should enable MCD to remain popular even in an increasingly health-conscious American culture. In fact, if weight loss drugs become increasingly prevalent and other medical care advances take off through AI and other technological innovations, the downsides of eating relatively unhealthy fast food may decline as well, further encouraging consumption of MCD's products. Additionally, MCD itself may also innovate as needed to offer increasingly healthy options on its menus to continue to attract and retain customers should this play out.

Given these factors, I think MCD is an underappreciated AI opportunity on its most recent dip and rate it a Buy, even if its yield is not high enough to make its way into our portfolios. Instead, we are pursuing other higher-yielding, high-growth AI-related opportunities in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Synchrony Financial: Upgrading The Customer Base Pays Off
data.description:
Synchrony Stock Is Steady, Like Their Customers

Synchrony Financial (NYSE:SYF) stock has been basically flat since I downgraded it from Strong Buy to regular Buy last quarter. At that time, the stock had completed a strong run from the April Tariff Tantrum lows to break out above $70. Worries about tariffs impacting consumer purchasing turned out to be unfounded. With Synchrony's Q3 results now out, we see that consumer spending is even starting to pick up a bit. Credit quality has also improved, based on the decline in delinquencies and charge-offs.

With these results, the stock performance suggests that the market is not overly concerned about more cautious macro signals about the health of the consumer. This is reasonable when we remember that about a year ago, Synchrony started tightening credit standards and increasing rates to prepare for an expected cap on late fees that never materialized under the new administration. However, because of macro uncertainty, the bank has not yet rolled back any of these policy and pricing changes, leaving Synchrony with a higher-quality customer base.

Along with higher purchase volumes and credit quality, Synchrony is also benefitting from increasing net interest margins as Fed Funds rate cuts allow the bank to reduce rates on deposits while rates charged on loans are still climbing. As a result, Synchrony is posing higher net interest income despite loan balances and the number of accounts still being down slightly year-on-year.

Longer term, Synchrony will need to get back to loan growth. One lever is to loosen credit standards again, which the bank can do when the macro outlook is less cloudy. The other lever is Synchrony's retail partner relationships, which the bank is always trying to grow. The Walmart (WMT) OnePay co-branded card program is off to a good start. This quarter, Synchrony announced it is acquiring the commercial co-branded card program for professional customers of Lowe's (LOW).

With these growth drivers, I am predicting Synchrony will beat 2024's record EPS this year despite no growth in loan balances. My first look at 2026 predicts even better growth with continued strong net interest margins and a resumption of modest loan balance growth. Synchrony continues to aggressively reduce share count with buybacks, also boosting EPS growth. With P/E ratios of 8.2 times 2025 earnings and 7.6 times 2026 earnings, the common stock is still a Buy. Conservative or income-seeking investors may also consider Series A (NYSE:SYF.PR.A) preferred shares. Series A has a fixed rate and capital appreciation potential as market interest rates come down. Series B (NYSE:SYF.PR.B) preferred shares have a higher current yield but is subject to a reset to lower rates or being called in 2029.

Purchase Volume Returns To Growth And Net Interest Margin Improves Strongly

While accounts and loan balances have not yet returned to year-on-year growth in Q3 after Synchrony tightened credit standards, purchase volume is growing again. This indicates customers are spending more.

It might be tempting to pin the purchase volume growth on inflation, but that is not the case. Customers are using their cards more often. Transaction frequency is showing accelerating growth, while the average amount per transaction only recently turned positive.

Net interest margin growth accelerated in Q3 as well, with both the cost of funds and the yield on loans improving in Synchrony's favor. Interest-bearing liabilities cost (mostly deposits) declined by 49 basis points, and yield on loans improved by 29 basis points. NIM for Q3 was 15.62%, and Synchrony is now guiding for a 2H 2025 average NIM of 15.7%.

Credit Quality Improvement Accelerates

Whether we look at delinquencies, charge-offs, or loss allowance, the year-on-year decline for Q3 was greater than that in Q2, indicating accelerating credit quality improvement.

So far in 2025, Synchrony's loss reserve has decreased despite a $45 million build for the Lowe's professional card portfolio. Given the approximately $800 million loan balance for this new program, the incremental loss allowance of 45/800, or 5.63%, shows that Synchrony is adding higher-quality accounts than average. For the full year, Synchrony has improved their guidance for charge-offs to 5.6-5.7% of loans, the lower half of the previous guidance range.

We normally expect Retailer Share Arrangement costs to grow with improved credit quality as Synchrony allows its retail partners to share in the gains, but RSA guidance for the year was trimmed slightly to 3.95-4.05%, from 3.95-4.10% previously.

Model Results And Valuation

Compared to last quarter's forecast, the improved net interest margin and provision for losses more than offset higher operating costs. I also increased my predicted buybacks to $667 million per quarter from $500 million previously, as the board has authorized an incremental $1 billion total through H1 2026. This takes my 2025 EPS prediction to $8.79, a 2% improvement from last quarter's forecast.

I am rolling out my first pass at 2026 this quarter. This is entirely my estimate, as the company has not yet provided guidance for next year. A key driver is modest loan balance growth of 2%. About 80 basis points of this comes from the acquisition of the Lowe's professional card program, and the rest is organic. Additionally, I am assuming net interest margin remains at the H2 2025 level, around 15.7%. The Fed is expected to cut rates twice next year, lowering deposit rates. However, I expect interest rates on card balances to start declining as well. RSA costs and charge-off rates in 2026 are also projected to be similar to 2H 2025. I am projecting a slight increase in costs, but I still see the efficiency ratio improving to 32.4% next year. Buybacks of $667 million per quarter continue through Q2. With these changes, 2026 EPS is projected to be $9.51, growth of 8.2% from 2025, and a forward P/E of 7.6.

Synchrony is still a Strong Buy on the Seeking Alpha quant rankings, and the score of 4.84 out of 5 is unchanged from last quarter. It is ranked 3rd out of 38 stocks in the Consumer Finance industry, but its two closest domestic peers are highly ranked as well. Capital One (COF) is ranked 2nd, and Ally Financial (ALLY) is ranked 6th. Looking at 2025 P/E ratios, Synchrony is the cheapest of the 3, although Ally is expected to catch up with stronger earnings growth in 2026.

The balance sheet shows the rapid reduction in share count through buybacks, going from 406.9 million shares at the end of 2023 to a projected 332.3 million shares at the end of 2026. However, further buybacks are likely in the second half of 2026 beyond what is currently authorized. Without further buybacks, the common equity/asset ratio would end 2026 at 13.8%, overcapitalized compared to historic levels.

Synchrony also returns capital through dividends, but not as much as it does with buybacks. The current payout of $0.30 per quarter is a yield of 1.67%, and I am projecting an increase to $0.35 per quarter in 2Q 2026.

My 2025 year-end estimate of book value is $44.48, down about 1% from last quarter's projection due to the higher buybacks. This grows to 51.59 by the end of 2026, although higher buybacks would reduce this if authorized. That takes P/B to 1.62 for 2025 and 1.40 for 2026.

Preferred Share Update

Since last quarter, the Series A preferred shares (NYSE:SYF.PR.A) continued to outperform Series B (NYSE:SYF.PR.B) on a total return basis.

This would be expected given the decline in the 5-year treasury yield (US5Y) to 3.63% from 3.91% last quarter. Remember that Series B will reset in May 2029 to a coupon rate of 4.044% plus the 5-year treasury yield. That formula would predict a dividend cut for Series B to a 7.674% from 8.25% currently (based on a par value of $25). Series A has a fixed 5.625% coupon, so lower rates will do nothing to the dividend amount but are more likely to cause the share price to increase than they are for Series B.

Using market prices of $20.29 for Series A and $26.35 for B, current yields are 6.93% for A and 7.83% for B. The 90 basis point spread has widened slightly since last quarter. Series B carries the risk of a lower reset in 2029, as well as the risk that Synchrony elects to call it at $25. Series A is already subject to call at any dividend payment date but is trading well below par and is unlikely to be called given the low coupon rate.

Despite the BB- rating by S&P, the preferred shares look safe, with only $83 million of dividends per year compared to net income around $3.3 billion.

Conclusion

Synchrony Financial common shares have been stable over the last quarter despite lingering macro worries about consumer spending. Synchrony upgraded its consumer base in the past year by tightening lending standards. As a result, the bank is not seeing any slowdown in spending. Delinquencies and charge-offs have even improved. Loan balance and active accounts have stagnated this year, but the bank is on track to improve next year with the addition of the Lowe's Pro commercial card portfolio. Declining deposit rates and steady credit card rates have improved net interest margin, and Synchrony continues to be a massive buyback machine, further boosting EPS.

With growth expected in 2026 and P/E still cheap around 8, the common shares remain a Buy. Series A Preferred is also still a Buy with more capital appreciation potential as rates decline. Series B is downgraded to Hold as it is subject to call risk or a reset lower in the dividend in 2029.

data.title: Figma: Lower AI Risk Plus Momentum Equals Extreme Valuation
data.description:
Figma (NYSE:FIG) made a lot of noise in an erupting IPO, with shares more than tripling on the first day of trading, surpassing a $55 billion valuation, nearly three times the price Figma agreed to get acquired at by Adobe (ADBE).

After the IPO fireworks stopped, shares pretty much moved in only one direction, now down nearly 50% from the peak.

Still, Figma is very richly valued, at over 30 times EV/Sales, which is curious considering the weakness we're seeing in the software sector, with peers like Adobe, which are incomparably profitable, trading at a fraction of the price.

This leads to a big debate regarding Figma - Is it a designer operating system that will benefit greatly from AI? Or is it just another tool that's at risk of disruption?

Even if the answer is favorable, I argue that Figma's valuation is just too tough to swallow.

Introduction To Figma

Figma launched its first product for public release in late 2016 and has since taken the designer world by storm. The idea behind Figma was to utilize the web to build a real-time collaboration surface, replacing the old ways of pathing around prototypes during the development and design process.

With Figma, the person in charge of the product can check the designer's work without interfering, give their input, and perhaps do some minor changes. Further, Figma has plenty of tools that allow faster and easier work, replacing legacy tools like Photoshop in many cases.

Figma offers an expansive suite of solutions, also utilizing AI, with products like Figma Make that turn prompts into prototypes and apps; Figma Draw for freeform creation; Figma Sites for website design, and many more.

Today, multi-billion dollar companies build with Figma, from Affirm (AFRM), to Netflix (NFLX), Coinbase (COIN), or Nubank (NU). It has nearly 12,000 customers with $10,000+ of ARR, and 1,119 customers with more than $100,000 in ARR.

In short, Figma is where many of the front-end use cases are being built, and where much of the user interfaces you're encountering in your daily lives are being created.

The Death Of Software

If you've been following the market since the launch of ChatGPT in late 2022, you couldn't have missed the market's growing concern about software in the age of AI.

This 'Sell Software' narrative has been up and down year-to-date, but for now, prominent software names are in the midst of sharp drawdowns.

This narrative boils down to two threats. One, AI will make it so easy to write software that it will replace it, or at least create downward pricing pressure. Two, AI will reduce the need for employees, thereby hurting companies that charge on a per-seat basis.

It is that narrative that's causing companies like Adobe, Salesforce (CRM), and others to trade at historically low valuations.

The question that arises, therefore, is where Figma falls under this narrative.

Operating System Vs. Designer Tool

In my opinion, the size of the AI threat for each software company is determined by its product's a) complexity, b) necessity, c) switching costs, and d) value creation.

For example, Microsoft's (MSFT) Excel is a complicated tool with too many features to count. For its users, Excel is a necessary tool, and for most companies, the switching costs are immense. Just thinking about moving my own humble folder of valuation models from Excel to Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) Sheets without harming the data and structures, I get a headache. Think about what it means for a company, let alone a big one. This is also a reflection of the value of Excel; I simply cannot do my job without it.

Figma's unparalleled collaboration infrastructure and extensive features suite make it very hard to develop internally.

It's also the type of solution that directly leads to outcomes for customers. The number of employees using Figma isn't correlated with the value it provides to a specific customer, who, at the end of the day, needs to create an app or a website. As such, a reduction in seats will likely be offset by value-based pricing.

Another important point is that Figma has become a work process as much as it is a tool, which means replacing it would require a complete change of the way things are being done in your company.

Over time, what we're seeing is that more and more use cases are coming inside Figma, making it almost like an operating system.

To me, it's much more likely that OpenAI (OPENAI) or Anthropic APIs will be utilized inside Figma, where iteration and development can be done, rather than Figma use cases going out to ChatGPT or similar solutions.

Strong Financials Overshadowed By Deceleration Outlook

In the last announced second quarter, Figma revenues were an all-time high of $250 million, up 41% Y/Y, and nearly 10% Q/Q, marking a deceleration from the 46% Y/Y increase in Q1, but an acceleration from the 5% growth Q/Q.

Net dollar retention was 129% in Q2, down from 130%+ in each of the past four quarters. This is an important metric, but not as important as gross dollar retention, which companies tend not to disclose. Insight Partners cofounder, Jeff Horing explains that here. In short, net dollar retention can mask significant churn, as long as your big customers increase their spending.

That is why Figma's disclosure of paid customer counts is key and very impressive. As of Q2, Figma had 11,906 customers with more than $10,000 in ARR, and 1,119 customers with more than $100,000, up 31% and 42%, respectively. For both metrics, this was one of the strongest quarters in terms of net additions.

As growth continues to be strong, Figma is also maintaining extraordinary gross margins above 90%, though the addition of AI solutions seems to be hurting it a little bit. Still, 90% gross margins are almost unheard of.

Below the gross margin line, it's hard to parse out the run-rate profitability for the company due to fluctuations in stock-based comp and several non-recurring items, so I think we should just look at their outlook.

For 2025, Figma guided revenues of a little over $1 billion, and non-GAAP operating profits of $93 million at the midpoint, reflecting 9% operating margins.

Meanwhile, for Q3, guidance is looking at $264 million in revenues, which would mean a 33% increase Y/Y, and a 6% increase Q/Q.

With a company like Figma, which is still relatively small, investors should closely monitor the second derivative. In other words, they should focus on the growth of growth. On that note, an 800 bps deceleration from Q2 is a cause for concern.

Valuation

Here's when things become problematic. At $64 a share, Figma trades at a valuation that plays in the field of the Cloudflares (NET), CrowdStrikes (CRWD), and Shopifys (SHOP), i.e., the most richly valued companies in the market (except for Palantir (PLTR), which is in a different league).

What those other companies have in common is a long track record of hypergrowth at scale. The smallest of them being Cloudflare, which is twice as big in terms of sales.

Personally, I simply cannot invest in companies at such valuation levels, but I believe the case for Figma is even harder.

Figma doesn't have the track record of growth at a large scale, and it's already expected to decelerate. It's also extremely far from a valuation that's connected to its earnings (current EV/EBIT is 324x).

For this valuation to make any sense, investors need to underwrite many years of 25%+ growth, alongside improving margins, with very little tangibles to rely on. The downside here is way too big, even though the company is great.

Conclusion

Figma should be on investors' watchlists, as a unique company with huge potential, evidenced by its 90%+ gross margins, strong retention numbers, and fast growth.

However, its current valuation requires a VC-like approach to investing, and doesn't make sense in a public equities portfolio.

The downside risk here is extreme, with no earnings to provide a floor, and a sales multiple that's one of the highest in public markets. If guidance misses ever so slightly, the stock could easily fall 20%.

As such, I initiate Figma at a 'Sell'.

That said, stocks like these trade on momentum, and after they set expectations low for Q3, I believe there is a path for momentum to keep going for a while.

data.title: Buy The Dip For This 10% Dividend Yield: Ares Capital
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

A narrative is a powerful thing in the stock market, and since September, we've seen BDCs (Business Development Companies) on a downward trend. Declining interest rates and fears of credit defaults have been spurred by the bankruptcy filing of First Brands amid accusations of financial irregularities and possibly fraud. This followed on the heels of another lender in the auto business, Tricolor, also being accused of a significant fraud scheme.

Does it matter that most BDCs had minimal to no exposure to these? No. Fear doesn't need logic. Forget throwing out the baby with the bathwater; the market will just tear down the whole house.

Fortunately, earnings season is here, and with earnings season, we can get a clear picture of what is actually happening with specific companies. When a whole sector is selling off, the best thing to do is look at some of the highest quality options and take advantage of the lower prices.

ARCC: A Blue-chip BDC

Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC), yielding 9.6%, is a premium BDC that focuses on the upper-middle market. The last time we talked about ARCC was after earnings, where we said:

"ARCC is a BDC that definitely deserves to trade at a premium to NAV. Given its size and its slow and steady growth, that premium should be in the 110-120% range in our opinion. That range tends to align with where ARCC's price typically trends toward in the long run. At current NAV, that is a range of $21.89 to $23.88. If it is trading above that range, we'd recommend being patient. If it is below that range, then it's a bargain worth going out of the way for."

Good things come to those who wait, with ARCC now trading at a tiny premium to NAV (less than 1% based on Q2 2025 NAV). This is part of a broader trend down in BDCs as the market views a lower interest rate environment as less favorable.

On the one hand, we understand the concern. ARCC makes money by lending at floating rates. ARCC reports that 69% of its assets are floating rate as of Q2 2025, which is consistent with its historical mix: Source

How Lower Rates Impact ARCC

When we consider ARCC's earnings, they are even more exposed to floating rates since 11% of their portfolio is equity and other non-income-producing investments, and another 7% is invested in Ivy Hill Asset Management, which in turn also has significant exposure to floating-rate assets.

If interest rates are cut, the interest that floating-rate assets pay declines directly and immediately. In ARCC's 10-Q, we can see their interest rate sensitivity. Source

A 100 bps decrease in interest rates is expected to reduce ARCC's net income by $121 million, or approximately $0.17/share per year. We've seen 25 bps so far, and another 25 bps cut is widely expected in October. A potential of a third 25 bps cut in December is also the dominant opinion, according to the Fed Futures market. While the futures market can be wrong, the current expectation is that there will be a total of 75 bps to close out 2025. That is a good $0.12-$0.13 headwind to earnings for ARCC and a headwind for most other BDCs.

Investors who are selling might also be worried about credit quality, a potential recession, and other factors. But the very simple math of declining interest rates reducing the interest paid by floating-rate investments is arguably the strongest motivation to sell today.

While others are selling, we're looking at ARCC trading near Q2 NAV and quite possibly below where NAV is today and salivating. Why?

Note that the chart above states "assuming no changes in our investment and borrowing structure". There is more than one variable at work. Declining interest rates are a headwind for BDCs, but there are solutions. One solution is leveraging up.

Leveraging Up to Earn More

ARCC has been operating at a very modest leverage level of 0.98x debt/equity.

If we go back to say December of 2021, ARCC was at 1.21x debt/equity. ARCC has ample room to increase its investments by several billion using 100% debt if it wants to. Higher leverage leads to higher cash flow for the equity. Obviously, we don't want ARCC taking on a level of debt that is too risky, but certainly leveraging up to the 1.10x-1.20x range is well within their historical norms and isn't overly greedy.

Last earnings call, CEO Schnabel noted that operating at a low leverage level created flexibility in the future:

"Obviously, with the volume being a little bit lighter than we had hoped in this past quarter, we weren't able to get more into leverage. But again, core earnings of $0.50 a share still feels very good and stable and well covering the dividend. And so it doesn't really bother us that we are operating around 1x leverage. In fact, it probably just gives us a lot of financial flexibility going forward to take advantage of whatever kind of market environment we encounter."

In September, we saw ARCC issue $650 million in new bonds, noting in their press release that the proceeds were being used to repay their revolving line of credit. This is usually a good signal that they have been deploying capital during the quarter. Given that ARCC was trading at a premium to NAV early in Q3, we expect that they likely issued equity during the quarter as well.

Conclusion

Interest rates being cut is a headwind to earnings, but many seem to be ignoring that growth is a tailwind to earnings. Additionally, as interest rates decline, we expect that demand for investing at higher yields will increase. Let's not forget that in 2021, ARCC's share price hit an all-time high in a zero percent interest world, and the dividend was 17% lower than it is today. Long-term, we don't expect lower rates to be a significant headwind to the share price, even if the market's knee-jerk reaction is to sell today.

In terms of credit quality, ARCC is one of a handful of publicly traded BDCs that went through the Great Financial Crisis. This is a manager who knows how to navigate challenging environments and come out the other side strong. So, when the whole sector shows weakness, ARCC is a go-to pick for me.

Being a premium BDC, ARCC often trades at a significant premium to NAV. Today, it trades at a discount to NAV. We're grabbing more shares before earnings (scheduled for October 28th).

When the market gets these negative narratives, look for the opportunity it is leaving behind and seek the highest quality investments that might be falling in sympathy.

data.title: AI Spending Is Powering The Economy - Dividend Stocks To Buy Before The Next Wave
data.description:
Billionaire investor and leader of the world’s largest alternative asset manager, Blackstone (BX), Stephen Schwarzman recently described the scale of AI infrastructure spending in the United States as "stunning" and, in fact, becoming so large that it is actually powering the U.S. economy.

The numbers show that Mr. Schwarzman is correct, as AI capex in the first half of 2025 accounted for about 110 basis points of U.S. GDP growth. While the near-term impact of AI-related spending is evident in the GDP numbers, it naturally raises the question of how sustainable this trend really is. Ultimately, this depends on how profitable AI will become for the broader economy, because if it fails to deliver an adequate ROI, the spending could dry up as quickly as it grew. This article will examine that question and then share ways that dividend investors can allocate capital to profit while also generating attractive and sustainable income.

AI Spending is Powering the U.S. Economy

According to the Bureau of Economic Analysis, U.S. non-residential fixed investments increased by 7.3% on an annualized basis in the second quarter of this year, and that growth was almost entirely due to investments in AI-related infrastructure and data centers. The growth trend is unlikely to halt anytime soon, as Goldman Sachs (GS) reported recently that data center construction spending has tripled over the past three years alone, indicating rapid acceleration of capex in this sector.

This surge is largely driven by major megacap tech companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Meta (META), which are all accelerating their AI spending. Taken together, those four companies are expected to spend over $320 billion on AI initiatives this year alone, representing massive growth over the past several years.

Additionally, AI-driven investment may add up to 1.2 percentage points to U.S. GDP growth this year, helping to prop up the economy in the face of weakening numbers from traditional sectors like manufacturing, retail, and housing. This indicates that the U.S. economy is shifting increasingly toward an AI-powered future, meaning its long-term health is becoming ever more dependent on AI fulfilling its promise.

Schwarzman’s Warning: A Boom That Must Pay for Itself

It is also important to keep in mind that Schwarzman's comments here are not purely theoretical estimates based on news reports, but rather what he is seeing within his own vast business empire, given that BX manages well over a trillion dollars' worth of assets and businesses across the globe with a particular focus on the U.S. economy. As such, it is important to take to particular heart his point that there seems to be a major investment boom that is propping up the U.S. economy and that its ultimate impact will be either one that puts the U.S. ahead of virtually every other country in the world except perhaps China as the global AI superpower or results in a colossal bust due to the misallocations of hundreds of billions or even trillions of dollars with minimal ultimate returns.

This also means that investors need to be increasingly circumspect about the returns on the capital they are deploying to AI, given that most AI opportunities today are bid up very highly, thereby requiring that the AI achieve its full potential in order to receive a satisfactory return on that investment. Thus, the U.S. economy right now is transitioning from being consumption-driven, given that consumer data is weakening, towards being a major infrastructure investment-driven economy with incredible amounts of money going into data centers, power grids, power generation, and AI R&D.

This trend bodes well for AI infrastructure companies like NextEra Energy (NEE), Vistra (VST), Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC), and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC), as well as natural gas midstream leaders such as Enbridge (ENB), The Williams Companies (WMB), and Kinder Morgan (KMI). It is also important to keep in mind that this sort of build-out is placing significant strain on capital markets, such that if interest rates rise materially from here, it could put considerable strain on the ability of the economy to sustain these sorts of investments. At the same time, interest rates must be high enough to attract sufficient capital investment, and therefore, the Fed artificially cutting rates at a point in the U.S. economic cycle where massive investment is needed to drive this sort of build-out could be counterproductive by encouraging consumers instead to shift their capital towards borrowing and spending, which would only worsen conditions.

Where Dividend Investors Can Profit From the AI Build-out

With all this said, how should income investors deploy capital to take advantage of it? In my view, finding places along the infrastructure build-out chain that still trade at reasonable or attractive valuations is a great way to play it. Since going directly after the big AI players like NVIDIA (NVDA), Palantir (PLTR), or Meta generally means paying a very high price for the exposure you get.

Instead, I think midstream (AMLP) names with some natural gas exposure, like the ones already mentioned, as well as Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and MPLX (MPLX), could be very attractive risk-adjusted investments, as would investments in reasonably priced power infrastructure companies (UTG) like NEE and BEP. Investors can also gain exposure to AI-related infrastructure through data center-focused REITs such as Digital Realty Trust (DLR) and Equinix (EQIX) or even through specialized data center developers and asset managers like Blue Owl Capital (OWL).

Additionally, hedging against a market collapse in the event of an AI bust could involve investing in precious metals like gold (GLD) and silver (SLV), and in particular bullion and leases that throw off an attractive yield, while providing full upside from the underlying bullion itself could also make sense.

Investor Takeaway

Ultimately, it is hard to know exactly how productive AI is going to be and how soon if it fulfills its full promise or even potentially exceeds it. However, there is a very real downside risk for leading AI stocks at current valuations, as evidenced by the fact that fund managers are growing increasingly wary that valuations may be approaching bubble-like levels.

Therefore, I think a safer bet is buying fairly undervalued infrastructure stocks that are positioned to benefit from the AI boom but should not be totally devastated if it fails to deliver on its promise. Additionally, by hedging via gold bullion leases, investors can also generate attractive income for their portfolios while hedging against the economic fallout that would undoubtedly result from a disappointing outcome for AI. As a result, these are two of our largest positions in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: RWL: A Smart Way To De-Risk Your Mega-Cap Exposure
data.description:
Many investors of the S&P 500 are worried about the high level of concentration of the index due to the market-cap weighting methodology of the index, significantly overweighting key holdings. Some ETFs are trying to take different approaches to mitigate this concentration, and the ETF that I will analyze today is one of them. The Invesco S&P 500 Revenue ETF (NYSEARCA:RWL) is weighting companies by revenues instead of market capitalization, and this results in a very different portfolio that is not taking investor sentiment into account.

I rate the fund a buy as I think it can be highly relevant for diversification purposes, however I do not think that it should be considered as a replacement for the S&P 500

A Fundamental Approach to Weighting

The Invesco S&P 500 Revenue ETF is tracking the S&P 500® Revenue-Weighted Index. The basis for the thesis of this ETF is the observation that the top constituents of the S&P 500 are becoming increasingly important in the index, and thus looking at ways to diversify weights is also becoming increasingly important, as you can see from the graph below:

By weighting companies by topline, you will see below that this is a very effective way to build a very different portfolio, while still investing in the same companies as the one you will have with the S&P 500 and an ETF such as the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), the largest ETF tracking the index and that we will use as a benchmark for this analysis. RWL takes the last twelve months sales quarterly to rebalance holdings, a strategy that keeps a close eye on companies earning reports and not on price momentum. This is an interesting strategy, but results in a portfolio favoring companies with high sales figures, not necessarily high cash generation or growth prospects, and this is what I think is more relevant as a complement than as a core holding.

The quarterly rebalancing of the portfolio results in a higher portfolio turnover than the S&P 500 despite investing in the same companies. The portfolio turnover is 15% against 3% for VOO. A high turnover typically means higher costs for the fund, but I think that 15% remains a reasonable figure, and is necessary given the strategy of the fund to keep track of every earnings in each quarter. This is consistent with the approach, and the fact that it is “only” 15% still highlights that there is a consistency in the way that the portfolio is built. RWL is not focused on the popularity of each company by investors, but by their economic footprint, and right now, the economic environment is shifting rapidly.

A Reshuffling Away from Tech Dominance

A direct consequence of the fund’s weighting methodology is the very different portfolio, both in terms of sectoral splits and individual stocks. If you have the S&P 500’s top 10 holdings in mind, you will be surprised to see the top 10 holdings of RWL:

You can clearly see that the weights are very different from the concentration of the S&P 500. The top 3 holdings are accounting for 9.84% against 20.94% for VOO: more than double. This is also true for the top 10 concentration: 24.08% against 37.85%.

If you look closely at the top 10, you will see how uniquely positioned the fund is, and how different the drivers of the fund are with regard to the S&P 500. The two largest holdings of the fund are Walmart (WMT) and Amazon (AMZN), two larg-cap companies with significative revenue metrics giving you direct exposure to American consumption and exposure ot the surge of e-commerce acitivites. They are driven by consumer spending and operational efficiency. This is followed by Health Care players UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and McKesson (MCK) and CVS Health (CVS): differents steps of the value chain. These three are reaffirming my constatation that RWL is a defensive player, as the revenue of these companies are benefiting from an inelastic demand and strong long term drivers like demographic shifts. Of course, as they are giants, you are still going to find tech players such as Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT), but in much lower weights than in the S&P 500, meaning that their performance will not be dominating the fund's outlook. This allows the fund's performance to leave rooom for cyclical value companies such as Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) and Exxon Mobil (XOM). Overall, compared to the S&P 500, it appears that the portfolio of RWL is more directly tied to the economic activity, consumer health, and healthcare trends, rather than the investor sentiment for tech companies.
